title: Toro y Moi Cannot Be Categorized and Hole Erth Proves It
author: Allastair Voss
description: Chazwick Bundick has spent his career making it impossible for anyone to predict what a Toro y Moi album will sound like. Chillwave. House. Funk. Lo-fi
image_alt: Toro y Moi Cannot Be Categorized and Hole Erth Proves It
body:
[spotify:artist/6O4EGCCb6DoIiR6B1QCQgp]

Chazwick Bundick has spent his career making it impossible for anyone to predict what a Toro y Moi album will sound like. Chillwave. House. Funk. Lo-fi pop. R&B. Each record has shifted the coordinates while maintaining something ineffably Chaz about the whole thing.

Hole Erth, his eighth studio album, released in September 2024 on Dead Oceans, is perhaps the most extreme pivot yet. Pop-punk energy. Autotuned rap. Collaborations with Don Toliver, Kevin Abstract, and Benjamin Gibbard of Death Cab for Cutie. If you came in expecting Causers of This Part Two, Hole Erth will disorient you. That is the point.

## What Hole Erth Actually Is

The album does not waste time announcing itself. The opening track establishes a compressed, distorted energy that has nothing to do with the warm bedroom-pop haze of Causers of This or the gleaming studio sheen of Outer Peace. Bundick here sounds like someone who has spent time listening to the Internet, to Brockhampton, to the kind of maximalist collage that defines early 2020s alternative rap and pop-punk crossover, and has decided to pursue that territory without apology.

Don Toliver's appearance on the album is not a guest spot in the conventional sense. It is a genuine collaboration, with Bundick building production that meets Toliver's melodic approach rather than asking Toliver to step into a Toro y Moi aesthetic. The result feels genuinely hybrid. Benjamin Gibbard brings his particular brand of open-hearted melody to his contribution, and it sits in the record without irony, which takes a certain confidence from Bundick. Mixing the earnestness of Death Cab for Cutie with the harder-edged rap elements elsewhere on the album could have produced tonal whiplash. Instead, the range feels intentional.

Kevin Abstract's presence connects Hole Erth to the post-Brockhampton moment in independent music, a space where genre distinctions mean less than emotional directness and where production style is understood as a statement in itself.

## Les Sins and the Side Projects

Bundick's side project Les Sins, his more explicitly dance-oriented alias, has been dormant since a joint EP with AceMo in 2020. The last full-length under that name was Michael in 2014. But the influence of Les Sins bleeds into everything Bundick touches. The rhythmic adventurousness, the willingness to let a groove dictate structure rather than verse-chorus convention, is present across all his work.

The fact that Les Sins has been quiet while Toro y Moi has absorbed its energy suggests that Bundick no longer needs the separation. The main project has become capacious enough to hold everything. Where an artist like Frank Ocean might construct separate identities for separate sonic moods, Bundick folds them into a single name and dares the audience to keep up.

Michael, the 2014 Les Sins record, remains one of the more underrated dance albums of the decade. Its blend of filtered house, post-disco, and vocal manipulation was clearly ahead of the curve for what independent producers would embrace several years later. Bundick essentially built a template that others took credit for popularizing.

## The Career Arc in Context

The arc from Causers of This in 2010 through Underneath the Pine, Anything in Return, What For?, Boo Boo, Outer Peace, Mahal, and now Hole Erth describes a career with no stable center, only a consistent restlessness. Each record resets the premise. Boo Boo was the most emotionally withdrawn and sonically murky record of his career, a deliberate step away from the accessibility of Anything in Return. Outer Peace was the corrective, bright and funky and unambiguous in its pleasure. Mahal expanded the palette toward psychedelic rock and world music influences, with Bundick playing most of the instruments and recording extensively outside the United States.

Hole Erth reads, in this context, as another full reset rather than a continuation of any specific thread. The pop-punk energy and the AutoTuned rap vocal style have no clear precedent in his earlier work. That discontinuity is the point. Bundick's audience has learned, over fifteen years, that loyalty to his catalog means accepting the next record might sound nothing like what came before.

## The Sandhills EP and the Bridge

Bundick also released the Sandhills EP in 2023, a smaller project that served as a bridge between the lush production of Mahal and the rawer energy of Hole Erth. The EP moved through folk and acoustic textures, suggesting that Bundick was deliberately stripping things back before building them up again in a different direction. This kind of transitional work is easy to overlook in a discography that moves quickly, but it matters. It reveals a methodical artist clearing space before committing to a new sound.

## The Tour and What Comes Next

A winter 2025 North American headline tour with Panda Bear as support ran through February and March. The pairing was pointed. Panda Bear, as a member of Animal Collective and as a solo artist, occupies adjacent territory in the independent music landscape, someone who has spent decades following his own aesthetic logic regardless of commercial expectation. Placing these two artists on the same bill was a statement about what independent music can look like when artists refuse to optimize for audiences they do not have.

An unplugged reworking of Hole Erth, titled Unerthed, was announced for later in 2025. The prospect of stripping back an album defined by compression and distortion into something acoustic or minimal is genuinely interesting. Bundick has always been a skilled melodist underneath the production choices, and Unerthed offers the chance to prove that the songs hold up on their own terms.

## The Career

Toro y Moi is one of the most consistently interesting artists in independent music. Not because every album is a masterpiece, but because every album is a genuine attempt to do something he has not done before. In a landscape where artists are rewarded for repeating themselves, Bundick's refusal to stay still is its own kind of excellence. At a point in his career when he could comfortably coast on nostalgia for the chillwave moment he helped define, he keeps moving. That is the rarer thing.
